text: Once Saul came back from chasing off the Philistines, word reached him that David was hiding in the wilderness near En-gedi.
So Saul picked three thousand men from all Israel and went looking for David and his followers near the rocks where the wild goats live.
Along the way he came to some sheep pens by a cave, and went inside alone to relieve himself — with David and his men hidden far back in that very cave.
David's men whispered that this was the day the LORD had promised to hand his enemy over to him; David crept up quietly and cut off a corner of Saul's cloak, unnoticed.
Afterward David felt his conscience strike him hard for cutting even that much off the king's cloak.
He told his men he would never lift a hand against Saul, since Saul had been set apart by the LORD as king.
With those words David held his men back and stopped them from attacking Saul as he left the cave and went on his way.
Soon after, David also came out, called after Saul, and bowed low with his face to the ground.
He asked Saul why he listened to people who claimed David meant him harm.
He pointed out that Saul had just seen with his own eyes how the LORD had placed him within reach in that cave, yet David spared him, saying he would not touch the LORD's anointed.
David held up the piece of cloak in his hand as proof he had cut it rather than killed him, though he had done Saul no wrong.
When David finished speaking, Saul asked, in tears, whether that was really David's voice.
Saul admitted David was the more righteous of the two, since David had repaid his cruelty with kindness.
Saul told David he now knew for certain that David would become king and that Israel's kingdom would be secure in his hand.
He asked David to swear that he would not wipe out his descendants or erase his family name afterward.
David gave Saul his oath, and Saul went back home while David and his men returned to their stronghold.
